w:r.w:t: Kung Fu Chess — Design Guide: Implementation and Tests
This document guides the teacher in leading students through the design and implementation of a
game using disciplined, test-driven, AI-assisted software engineering.
The goal is not only to finish a game. The real goal is to train students to:
Break a vague game idea into small, testable requirements.
Separate model, controller, real-time game logic, and rendering.
Write text-based integration tests that validate visible behavior.
Use an LLM as a junior development assistant, not as an uncontrolled authority.
Explain design decisions and prove correctness through tests.

The teacher uses this guide to steer the class. Students do not receive the full architecture as a finished solution on day one. The teacher reveals the next design boundary and the next test requirement at each iteration.
Every development step follows the same loop:
Requirement → test plan → failing tests → implementation → refactor → AI review → peer review → commit
This loop is more important than the specific game feature. The teacher stops students who jump directly into implementation without first making the behavior observable through tests.
Keep the game logic independent of the drawing library.
Write unit tests for pure logic before connecting UI.
Use fake clocks or explicit elapsed-time calls instead of real sleeping in tests.
Use small classes with clear responsibility.
Include a short explanation of each nontrivial design decision.
When using an LLM, students ask it for small, reviewable steps:
Propose the smallest class boundary for this behavior.
Review this diff for responsibility leakage.
Given the requirement that a rook moves horizontally and vertically until blocked, propose five unit tests before writing implementation.
Here is a failing test and the relevant class. Explain the likely boundary issue before suggesting a minimal change.
Compare putting pixel-to-cell mapping in Controller versus BoardMapper. Decide which keeps the model cleaner and why.
Students do not ask the LLM to
The teacher treats the LLM like a fast but unreliable junior developer: useful, but always reviewed by tests and humans.

Students first write their own short plan or diagnosis before asking the LLM to respond. The LLM may critique the plan, find missing cases, or compare alternatives, but it does not replace the student’s design responsibility.
Before accepting help, students ask the LLM to restate what it understood. If the restatement is wrong or vague, students correct the request before asking for code.
Students work one step at a time: first clarify the requirement, then choose the responsibility boundary, then write tests, then implement the smallest change, then review the diff.
When the goal is learning, students ask for a clue, a failing test, or the next diagnostic question instead of asking for the full answer.

When debugging with an LLM, students give exact reproduction steps, the expected behavior, the actual behavior, and the smallest relevant code or test. They also state which layer they suspect: Controller, GameEngine, RuleEngine, RealTimeArbiter, Board, Renderer, or TextTestRunner.
Before asking for a fix, students ask the LLM to identify which layer is most likely responsible and what evidence would confirm or reject that diagnosis.
If the LLM proposes several changes at once, students ask it to choose the smallest first change and the test that should fail before the change and pass after it.
Students ask the LLM to locate the likely responsibility boundary before asking for code. They must ask the LLM to explain why any suggested change is needed.

Students treat large unexplained rewrites as suspicious. If the LLM replaces a large amount of code with a small patch, students ask whether behavior was intentionally removed and which tests prove it remains covered.
After receiving an answer, students may ask the LLM to review its own answer for possible mistakes. The teacher still requires human review and passing tests.

Kung Fu Chess is a real-time chess-like game. Pieces move according to chess movement patterns, but moves are not instantaneous. A player selects a piece and clicks a destination. The piece travels toward that destination at a fixed speed.
For this course, the rules are fixed and intentionally simplified.
The common route is the required path for all students.
The board is any rectangular size inferred from a textual description.
Pieces are described using normal chess letters: K, Q, R, B, N, P.
Piece color is encoded by prefix: wK, bR, etc.
The game does not implement check, checkmate, castling, en passant, or promotion.
Capturing the opponent king ends the game.
A piece moves only according to its movement rule.
Sliding pieces do not pass through blocking pieces.
Tests use a fixed cell size in pixels.
UI rendering uses only the supplied drawing/image library.
The logical board changes only when a moving piece arrives.
The common route allows only one active motion at a time. If a legal-looking move is requested while a motion is already active, GameEngine rejects the request with reason
. No new motion starts and Board remains unchanged.
The text integration DSL contains only
is the only integration-test assertion mechanism.
The extra route is for students who finish the common route cleanly. The teacher chooses one extra feature per strong team. The allowed extra features are:
Cancellation when the destination becomes occupied before arrival.
Visual polish without changing the model.

The extra route must not damage the common route architecture. An extra feature is architecturally acceptable only when it extends the appropriate layer without reversing dependency direction or merging unrelated responsibilities. Cooldown belongs in movement or game-state coordination, not in the renderer. Replay belongs around command or event recording, not inside Board. Visual polish belongs in the renderer, not in the rules layer. If students cannot add the feature without rewriting the common route, the design is rejected and refactored first.
4. Core Design Decision: Split the System Into Testable Layers
The implementation is split into these layers:
— Board, positions, pieces, piece colors, piece types, and piece states.
— Legal destinations for each kind of piece.
— Validation of requested moves against the current board state.
— Movement over time, active motions, arrival resolution, capture events, and king-capture reporting.
— Translation of clicks into game commands.
— Drawing the current state using the supplied drawing library.
— Parsing textual board setup and printing logical board state.
— Execution of textual integration tests without a real UI.
Every layer must be testable without the layers above it. Layer ownership is fixed:
Board coordinates, piece identity, logical occupancy, and piece lifecycle state.
Pixels, clicks, rendering, script parsing, movement rules, or timing.
Movement geometry for each piece type, calculated from Board and Piece data.
Game commands, elapsed time, animation, rendering, or input handling.
Read-only legality validation for a requested move.
Board mutation, animation, click interpretation, or game-over state.
Application-service coordination: game-over guard, validation delegation, starting legal motions, wait delegation, and snapshots.
Piece-specific movement logic, rendering, input parsing, DSL parsing, or pixel mapping.
Active Motion objects, simulated time advancement, arrival resolution, and capture events.
Chess legality, clicks, rendering, or script parsing.
Chess legality, Board mutation, rendering, or timing.
Visual drawing from a read-only GameSnapshot.
Game rules, Board mutation, input parsing, or text-test logic.
Script parsing and driving the public command path.
Movement rules, direct Board mutation, or duplicated game logic.
BoardParser and BoardPrinter: textual setup and logical board output.
Movement rules, command execution, rendering, or test assertions beyond text comparison.
The project uses this Python structure:
Controller depends on BoardMapper and GameEngine.
GameEngine depends on Board, RuleEngine, and RealTimeArbiter.
RuleEngine depends on Board and PieceRules.
PieceRules depends only on Board and Position data.
BoardParser and BoardPrinter depend on model data, but not on Controller, RuleEngine, RealTimeArbiter, or Renderer.
TextTestRunner depends on BoardParser, BoardPrinter, Controller, and GameEngine.
Model depends on nothing above it.
The direction of dependency never points from model to UI.

GameEngine is the application-service layer. It coordinates Board, RuleEngine, and RealTimeArbiter. It does not contain piece-specific movement logic, rendering code, input parsing, DSL parsing, or pixel mapping.
in the common route. A move request is represented by source and destination cells at the public API boundary. The engine returns a MoveResult; it does not require a separate model-level Move object.
Pattern vocabulary used by the teacher:
represents a board cell, not pixels.
is a value object. It does not know board size, rendering, movement rules, or input coordinates.
Two positions with the same row and column are equal.
Two positions with different row or column are not equal.
Position objects produce readable assertion failures.
kind: king / queen / rook / bishop / knight / pawn
is only a lifecycle flag: idle, moving, or captured. It does not store path, destination, elapsed time, speed, interpolation, or arrival logic. Those details belong to Motion and RealTimeArbiter.

Piece IDs are assigned consistently at creation time, either by BoardParser or by a dedicated PieceFactory. If stable identity is used for motion tracking or snapshots, duplicate piece IDs are invalid.
A piece never knows about the renderer, mouse clicks, pixels, or text test syntax.
owns the logical arrangement of pieces.
Query a piece at a cell.
Check whether a cell is inside bounds.
Move a piece after a move has been validated.
does not contain chess movement rules. The board knows what exists; it does not decide which chess moves are legal.
assumes validation has already happened. Board does not call RuleEngine, and RuleEngine does not mutate Board.
Occupied cells return the correct piece.
Adding two pieces to the same cell fails.
Moving a piece updates source and destination.
Removing a captured piece clears its cell.
Each piece type has a small rule class with this interface:
The method returns destinations based on piece movement and blocking. Enemy-occupied destinations may be legal destinations, but the rule class does not capture, remove, move, or mutate pieces.
Rook: Horizontal and vertical sliding until blocked.
Queen: Rook movement plus bishop movement.
King: One square in any direction.
White pawn moves one row upward.
Black pawn moves one row downward.
Pawn captures one diagonal step forward.
Pawn has no initial two-step move.
are stateless. They do not store selected pieces, active motions, elapsed time, or game-over state. They only calculate legal destinations from the given board and piece.
Unit tests are written per piece type:
Rook moves across empty row and column.
Rook stops before a friendly blocker.
Rook captures an enemy blocker but does not pass it.
Bishop moves diagonally and not straight.
Queen combines rook and bishop movement.
Pawn moves and captures according to the simplified pawn rules.
Given a source cell and a destination cell, is this command legal now?
Reject move attempts from empty cells.
Reject moving to a friendly-occupied destination.
Ask the relevant movement rule whether the destination is legal.
The common route does not implement check, pins, checkmate, castling, en passant, or promotion. The win condition is king capture.
is always present. For a valid move, it is
. For an invalid rule-level move, it is a stable machine-readable reason such as
is read-only with respect to Board. It inspects board state and returns
, but it does not move pieces, remove captures, start motions, or update game state.
Game-over is handled by GameEngine, not RuleEngine.
flag before calling RuleEngine. If the game is already over, GameEngine returns MoveResult with reason
The DSL does not assert these reasons. Unit tests assert them.
is the application-service coordinator. It is the public command boundary used by Controller and TextTestRunner.
Is another motion already active in the common route?
Should this requested move be delegated to RuleEngine for legality validation?
Should a validated move start a Motion through RealTimeArbiter?
Should simulated time be advanced through RealTimeArbiter?
What read-only GameSnapshot should be exposed to the renderer?
Hold or reference the current GameState, including the
when the common route already has an active motion, returning
Start a legal motion through RealTimeArbiter.
Receive king-capture notification from arrival resolution and set
Create GameSnapshot for the renderer and BoardPrinter.
GameEngine must not contain piece-specific movement logic, pixel mapping, rendering code, text parsing, or test-runner logic.
. For application-level rejection, common-route reasons include
. Rule-level invalid reasons are copied from MoveValidation.
. It is not mixed into the board, renderer, or controller.
RealTimeArbiter receives only already validated move commands and updates active motions as time advances.
Active motions are stored outside Board. Board represents logical occupancy only. RealTimeArbiter owns the collection of active Motion objects.
In the common route, RealTimeArbiter exposes whether any motion is active. GameEngine uses that fact to enforce the one-active-motion policy before starting a new motion.
Moving N squares takes N × 1000 ms.
Diagonal movement uses cell-step duration, not Euclidean pixel distance.
A bishop moving one diagonal square takes 1000 ms. A bishop moving three diagonal squares takes 3000 ms.
A moving piece remains logically on its source cell until it arrives. The board occupancy changes only on arrival.
This keeps one authoritative logical board state while still allowing the renderer to draw interpolated piece positions from snapshots.
The view can draw the piece between cells during movement. The logical board still changes only on arrival.
Remove it from the source cell.
Capture the enemy piece at the destination, when one exists.
Place the moving piece at the destination.
If the captured piece is a king, report the king capture to GameEngine.

Arrival resolution is atomic from the perspective of the rest of the system: remove from source, resolve capture, place at destination, update piece state, report king capture, and expose one consistent post-arrival state.
Tests never call real sleep. The engine exposes:
delegates time advancement to RealTimeArbiter. It must not directly manipulate Board motion state.
The controller translates user actions into game commands. It does not decide chess legality.
Convert pixels to board cells using
On first click, select a piece.
Clear selection after every second in-board click, whether the move is legal or illegal.
If no piece is selected, ignore clicks outside the board.
If a piece is selected, an outside-board click cancels the selection and sends no command to GameEngine.
Ignore first clicks on empty cells.
This selection policy is fixed so tests remain simple: outside-board clicks are ignored only when there is no selection; when there is a selection, they cancel it.
directly. It also must not call RuleEngine directly unless GameEngine intentionally exposes a validation-only query for UI feedback.
A click is outside the board when the mapped row or column is outside board bounds.
The common route has no scrolling camera. Pixel coordinates map directly to board coordinates.
Viewport support belongs only to an extra route. If added, the mapping is implemented inside
The view draws the game state. It does not own game rules and does not mutate the board.
The supplied drawing/image library is wrapped behind a small renderer interface.
The renderer receives a read-only snapshot:
View tests remain minimal. The main test coverage belongs to model, rules, controller, real-time movement, and text integration behavior.

The renderer receives GameSnapshot data only. It does not receive live Board or Piece objects, because live domain objects increase coupling and create opportunities for accidental mutation from the view layer.
The text-based tests are the visible specification of the game. They are also the strongest tool for keeping students focused.

BoardParser and BoardPrinter are text I/O adapters, not test-only helpers. ScriptRunner uses them, but they live outside texttests so the application and tests share the same board setup and board output behavior.
The DSL contains exactly four commands:
There are no extra DSL assertion commands.
Each row is written on a separate line.
Piece letters use chess notation: K, Q, R, B, N, P.
Board width and height are inferred from the text.
All rows must have the same number of cells.
simulates a user click in pixels.
advances simulated game time. It never calls real sleep.
is the only integration assertion mechanism.
, the script contains the exact expected logical board output. The script runner compares the actual board-printer output to those expected rows.
prints logical board occupancy, not animation pixel position.
The test passes only when the printed board exactly matches the expected rows after
The integration test suite uses exactly one assertion style:
This single assertion style covers all required common-route behavior:
Board parsing is tested by printing the initial board.
Pixel mapping is tested by clicking a piece and observing the resulting board after movement.
Legal movement is tested by observing the piece at the destination after enough time.
Partial real-time movement is tested by printing before arrival and after arrival.
Illegal movement is tested by printing an unchanged board.
Blocking is tested by printing an unchanged board after an attempted blocked move.
Capture is tested by printing a board where the captured piece disappeared.
King capture and game-over behavior are tested by attempting another legal-looking move after the king capture and printing an unchanged board.
Selection state is tested only by unit tests. The integration suite does not assert selection directly, because selection is not part of
Animation pixel position is tested only in renderer or snapshot unit tests. The integration suite does not assert animation position.
15. How Text Tests Connect to Implementation
The text test runner does not duplicate game logic. It performs the same actions a user performs:
It also does not own board parsing or board printing rules. It delegates those responsibilities to the shared text I/O adapters.
Board text -> BoardParser -> GameEngine initial state
The text runner never bypasses the controller for clicks and never bypasses the real-time arbiter for time.
This is wrong because it bypasses Controller, GameEngine, RuleEngine, and RealTimeArbiter. The test would no longer prove that the real user command path works.
The project requires both unit tests and text integration tests.
Is This Component Correct in Isolation?
rejects duplicate pieces in one cell.
completes a one-square move after 1000 ms.
selects a piece on first click and issues a move on second click.
Does the Feature Work From the Outside?
Given this board, when the user clicks a rook and clicks a destination, after enough time the printed board changes.
Given a blocked rook path, after the user tries the move, the printed board remains unchanged.
Given a king capture, after the user tries another move, the printed board remains unchanged because the game is over.
For each feature, students write several unit tests and one text integration test.
Unit tests do not replace integration tests. Integration tests do not replace unit tests.
Pure unit tests for legal destinations.
Unit tests for validation results and stable rule-level reasons.
Unit tests for game-over guard, validation delegation, mutation timing, and wait delegation.
Unit tests for elapsed time, active motions, arrival, capture events, and atomic resolution.
Unit tests with a fake GameEngine.
Unit tests for rectangular parsing, token validation, and stable logical output.
The course is organized as 11 iterations of 7 teaching hours each.
Each iteration ends with passing tests, a small demo, and a reviewed commit.
At the start of each iteration, students first write their own design plan: affected classes, responsibility boundary, tests to add, and expected public behavior.
Only after writing that plan do students ask the LLM to critique it for missing edge cases, responsibility leakage, over-engineering, and missing tests.
Iteration 0 — Project Skeleton, Text I/O, and Test Runner Skeleton
Goal: Create the repo, test framework, shared board parser/printer, and empty DSL runner.
Keep BoardParser and BoardPrinter in the shared io package, not hidden inside texttests.
BoardParser that creates a Board from text.
BoardPrinter that prints logical board occupancy.
Script runner that parses Board and print board for a trivial board.
Piece IDs are assigned at creation time by BoardParser or PieceFactory. Board rejects duplicate IDs if stable identity is required by the implementation.
Keep the model free of rendering.
Keep board coordinates separate from pixels.
as a lifecycle flag only; Motion owns movement details.
Board dimensions are inferred from text.
Board width and height are correct.
can become moving or captured, but contains no timing or destination data.
Goal: Connect clicks to board cells without moving pieces yet.
Keep input translation in the controller/input layer.
Keep the model free of pixels.
AI task: Ask the LLM to compare possible ownership of pixel-to-cell mapping and explain why BoardMapper owns it rather than Board or Piece.
Clicking an occupied cell selects that piece.
Clicking an empty cell with no selected piece does nothing.
Clicking outside the board with no selected piece does nothing.
Clicking outside the board while a piece is selected cancels selection and sends no move request.
A second in-board click sends a move request and clears selection.
Integration tests do not assert selection directly. Selection is verified by unit tests and by later movement integration tests.
Outside click is rejected by BoardMapper.
First click on a piece sets selected cell.
First click on an empty cell leaves selection empty.
Outside click with selected piece clears selection and does not call GameEngine.
Second in-board click sends the correct source and destination to the engine.
Goal: Implement the first piece movement rule.
Make rule tests pure and easy.
Do not implement all chess pieces at once.
Keep capture execution out of PieceRules.
AI task: Ask the LLM for missing rook-rule edge cases, then reduce them to the minimal tests required for blockers, enemy destinations, friendly destinations, and illegal directions.
Rook does not pass through blockers.
Rook includes an enemy blocker square as a legal destination.
No piece is removed in this iteration. This iteration proves capture eligibility, not capture execution.
Rook has correct legal destinations on an empty board.
Rook is blocked by a friendly piece.
Rook includes an enemy blocker as a legal destination.
Rook cannot pass an enemy blocker.
Iteration 4 — GameEngine Command Path Without Time
Goal: Make the click-click command path explicit before real-time movement is introduced.
Keep the controller from moving pieces directly.
Let GameEngine apply application-level guards and delegate legality to RuleEngine.
Second in-board click requests a destination.
GameEngine delegates normal move legality to RuleEngine.
Illegal command returns the stable RuleEngine reason and leaves Board unchanged.
Selection is cleared after the second in-board click.
This iteration does not move pieces, does not call
, and does not test arrival. The click-click-wait-print integration test belongs to Iteration 5.
Controller calls a fake GameEngine with correct source and destination.
Controller clears selection after valid and invalid second in-board clicks.
GameEngine delegates legal and illegal move requests to RuleEngine.
Invalid command does not mutate Board and does not start a motion.
Goal: Introduce movement over simulated time and complete the first click-click-wait-print vertical slice.
Use no real sleeping in tests.
Keep movement state separate from rendering.
Enforce one active motion in the common route.
AI task: Ask the LLM which state must remain outside Board during motion and which tests prove Board still represents only logical occupancy.
Before arrival, printed logical board remains unchanged.
While one motion is active, another requested move is rejected with reason
After 999 ms, piece has not logically arrived.
After 1000 ms for one square, piece arrives.
Partial wait followed by remaining wait equals one full wait.
delegates to RealTimeArbiter. Direct Board manipulation inside
GameEngine rejects a second move while RealTimeArbiter reports an active motion.
Iteration 6 — Captures and King Capture Win Condition
Goal: Implement capture execution and game over.
Capture happens on arrival, not on command issue.
Enemy piece at destination is removed on arrival.
Captured piece is marked captured if retained for snapshots or logs.
Friendly piece at destination makes the command invalid.
Capturing bK or wK ends the game.
After game over, further clicks do not change the board.
This test proves both king capture and game-over behavior using only
Non-king capture removes captured piece on arrival.
King capture sets game-over flag in GameEngine.
GameEngine rejects a command after game over with reason
Goal: Add bishop, queen, knight, king, and simplified pawn.
Start each new piece with unit tests.
Avoid testing every possible board through the DSL.
Queen combines rook and bishop movement.
King moves one cell in any direction.
Pawn follows the simplified pawn rules.
Write one text integration test that proves the piece works through click, wait, and print board.
Iteration 8 — Invalid Moves and Error Stability
Prevent bad input from corrupting state.
Keep validation reasons stable for unit tests.
AI task: Ask the LLM to list negative cases and classify each one by owner: Controller, GameEngine, RuleEngine, or RealTimeArbiter.
Clicking outside the board does not crash.
Outside click with selected piece cancels selection without calling GameEngine.
Moving from an empty cell is ignored by the controller path.
Illegal destination leaves the board unchanged.
Blocked sliding path leaves the board unchanged.
Moving to friendly-occupied destination leaves the board unchanged.
RuleEngine returns stable validation reasons for rule-level invalid cases.
GameEngine returns stable application-level reasons for
Invalid command does not start a motion.
Invalid command does not mutate board state.
Invalid command clears selection after the second in-board click.
Iteration 9 — Minimal Playable UI and Renderer
Goal: Build the smallest playable UI on top of the already-tested command path.
for input; do not implement a second input path inside the UI.
for drawing; do not pass live Board or Piece objects into Renderer.
Do not add new game rules in the renderer.
Draw moving pieces between cells based on snapshot pixel positions.
Highlight selected piece if the snapshot includes selection.
Snapshot contains enough data for rendering without exposing mutable Board or Piece objects.
Renderer smoke test draws a simple board without mutating game state.
A UI click is routed to
Iteration 10 — Refactoring, Review, and Extra Route
Goal: Harden the design and add one extra feature for strong teams.
AI task: Ask the LLM to review the diff for responsibility leakage, removed behavior, and missing regression tests before the teacher reviews it.
Add one extra feature only after the common route is complete and the minimal UI works.
Add text integration tests only when the behavior can be expressed through
Do not add new DSL assertion commands for the common course.
Before implementing an extra-route feature, students write a short architecture impact note:
Is the expected behavior visible through a unit test or
Did students write the expected behavior before the code?
Did students decide where the responsibility belongs?
Did students write their own plan before asking the LLM to critique it?
Was the AI question narrow enough to produce a reviewable answer?
Are students changing only the necessary classes?
Are students keeping rendering out of model logic?
Are students using fake time or explicit elapsed time?
Are students reading test failures carefully?
Are students verifying AI suggestions with tests before accepting them?
Is the diff small enough to review?
Is there a clear commit message?
Did students explain the design decision?
Did peer review or AI review catch anything useful?
Can students explain which AI suggestions they accepted, rejected, or changed, and why?
Mistake: Putting Pixel Coordinates in the Model
Correction: Pixels belong in BoardMapper, renderer, and animation snapshots. The model uses cells.
Mistake: Putting Chess Rules in the Controller
Correction: The controller only translates user intent. The RuleEngine decides legality.
Mistake: Making Tests Sleep in Real Time
Mistake: Implementing All Pieces Before Testing One Piece Well
Correction: Start with rook. Use it to prove the architecture.
Mistake: Using the UI to Test Logic Manually
Correction: Every visible behavior is reproducible through a text test.
Mistake: Relying on the LLM to Design Everything
Correction: Students ask the LLM for alternatives, tests, and edge cases. Students decide and test.
Mistake: Asking the LLM for the full answer when stuck
Correction: Ask for a clue, a failing test, the likely layer where the bug belongs, or the next diagnostic question. Students still write and verify the implementation.
is generated from logical game state, not screenshots.
Correction: The common course uses only
followed by expected rows. Other state is tested with unit tests.
Mistake: Making GameEngine the main object
Correction: GameEngine coordinates Board, RuleEngine, and RealTimeArbiter, but it does not contain piece-specific movement logic, rendering code, input parsing, DSL parsing, or pixel mapping.
Correction: RuleEngine validates only. GameEngine and RealTimeArbiter apply state changes at the correct time.
Mistake: Passing live Board or Piece objects into Renderer
Correction: Renderer receives GameSnapshot only and treats it as read-only.
Mistake: Storing active Motion objects inside Board
Correction: Board owns logical occupancy. RealTimeArbiter owns active motion state.
Correction: BoardParser and BoardPrinter are shared text I/O adapters. The text test runner uses them but does not own them.
Mistake: Starting a second common-route move while another piece is moving
Correction: Common route has one active motion at a time. GameEngine rejects the second request with reason
Mistake: Treating an outside-board second click as both ignored and selection-clearing
Correction: If no piece is selected, outside-board clicks are ignored. If a piece is selected, an outside-board click cancels selection and sends no command.
Mistake: Testing arrival before real-time movement exists
Correction: Iteration 4 tests command routing and validation only. Iteration 5 introduces
The project exposes this API between layers:
This API is intentionally small. Small interfaces are easier to test.
before delegating to RuleEngine. RuleEngine does not know about either application-level condition.
In the table, RTA means RealTimeArbiter.
May change controller selection and request a move.
May start a legal motion; rejects
Delegates simulated time advancement to RealTimeArbiter.
Advances simulated time and emits arrival events.
A feature is done only when:
Unit tests cover the internal logic.
At least one text integration test covers the user-visible behavior through
The implementation does not introduce UI dependency into the model.
Tests do not use real time.
The teacher can read the diff and understand the responsibility of each changed class.
The student can explain what they asked the LLM, what they accepted, and what they rejected.
The student can show the plan they wrote before asking the LLM and the tests used to verify any AI-assisted change.
The project feels like building a game, but the course is really about professional software construction.
How do we test this before seeing the UI?
What is the smallest failing test?
What could the LLM have missed?
What breaks when we add another piece type?
What breaks when we add animation, cooldown, or networking?
A good student solution is not the one with the most features. A good solution is the one where a new rule is added with a small test, a small implementation, and a small reviewable diff.
